-- 1 of 5 --

Tools, Part 2
OK, here’s part two of the tools, and in this video we're going to talk about list
management and shopping carts. As I mentioned in Part 1, it’s “horses for courses”.
There is no one solution that's going to work for everyone. Different tools have
different strengths. Different tools are right for different points in your business, and
your budget, and the sophistication you need.
These tools just keep on getting better but there's definitely no perfect tool, especially
in this area. In funnel pages and the page creation, those tools are more perfect than
these tools are, and everyone is always looking for a better solution. Everyone is asking
each other "who you using for a mailing list host?” Who are you using for a shopping
cart? Who are you using for affiliate management?" Everyone is always looking for
a better solution.
Moving solutions, moving to different tools is not a really pleasant thing, but it is
doable. I have stuck with the tools I've had for a very long time but I do know that with
other people it seems like every year they're switching to a new shopping cart. They all
have their own tutorials, so I'm not going to be giving you the step-by-step tutorials on
how to use these things. It'd be a little silly for me to try to create these. As you're
going to hear, some of the tools I recommend more highly than others. Some of them I
have affiliate links for and some I don't.
In terms of list management, I love Aweber (http://www.sixinseven.com/aweber)
or you can go to Profollow.com. Long story short, I love Aweber so much I had them
create a private label for me that's called Profollow.com. Then, after they kept
upgrading their service, I had them integrate profollow.com, so they are now identical
services, there's no difference between them. If you go to Profollow.com, I'll get a little
affiliate commission.
This is a tool that does one thing really well - it's where you host your lists. It sends
your emails out either as broadcasts or as autoresponder messages. I've been using it
for more than 10 years, it's a great service.
There's lots of different things to think about when you're looking at a list host, but the
number one thing is the deliverability: the ability for your email to land in someone's
inbox instead of in their spam folder and it's the constant battle that all the list
management services are always fighting to try to maintain their deliverability and
frankly, some do better than others. Every one of them has challenges at times, but my
experience and from talking to a lot of friends of mine that the best deliverability over
time - AWeber’s right up there at the top.
It's a great tool, it's also fast. When you hit send in your emails, even for a very large
list, the emails go out fast. If you build a list of tens of thousands of people, which I
hope you do one day (or maybe you're already there), with some list hosts, it might
take 4-6 hours to send that email. With Aweber, it takes minutes and that's a beautiful
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 2
PLF_Module1v1.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 2 of 5 --

thing because if you send at the end of a launch, and it's your last day and you're
closing in a few hours, you want that email to go out right away. You don't want it to
take four hours to go out, that would just not work. If you're sending an email that says
we're closing in three hours and that email takes six hours to get out, not good.
I've been with Aweber for over a decade, I'm obviously a fan. You can get there at
http://www.sixinseven.com/aweber. The nice thing about Aweber is that it's only $20/
month to start. As your list grows, the price goes up as you start to add more people,
but still as your list grows, your ability to make money from it grows. It's never going to
be a problem, with AWeber's pricing, to make a hundred times more than you're
spending with the service. Overall, big fan of Aweber.
MailChimp. I only put this in here because a lot of people ask about it and I think one
of the reasons they ask is because it's free to start with. You can build a small list there
for free. So, you can start there for free. I'm not a big fan of MailChimp. Some people
think it's easier to use. Frankly, I find the interface on Aweber easier to use, but maybe
that's because I'm used to it.
The thing with MailChimp is they have a pretty restrictive terms of service and they do
not allow you to send out affiliate promotions. Even if you're not thinking about sending
out affiliate promotions for other people now, that's fine, but what about three years
from now? You started building your list at MailChimp and now you want to send out a
promotion for someone and you can't just because of their terms of service. I am not a
huge fan of that.
I know a lot of people listening to this, you've probably already started building your list
at MailChimp and that's fine, you can stay there for now or you can move over to
Aweber. I would probably move over to Aweber if you've got the time and you're willing
to put in the effort. If you're just starting out, you can look at it and say well,
MailChimp's free and Aweber's $20/month. If you start building any size of a list at all,
then you're going to have to start paying at MailChimp. My advice is to start with a
professional service right off the bat and that's Aweber. I'm not saying MailChimp's not
a professional service, I'm just saying it's more restrictive in what you can do.
Don't be penny wise and pound foolish, just because it's going to cost you 20 bucks a
mont. Sure you can get a free account at MailChimp for a few months until your list
starts building up, but that's not a great reason to start there because the reality is
moving list hosts is not a fun thing to do. It's a pretty significant thing to do and if a few
years from now you've got 5000 people on your list and it costs you just as much at
MailChimp as at Aweber, and frankly I don't know if that's entirely true, but you'll
certainly by that time be paying the same for MailChimp. Dealing with their restrictive
terms of service is not a good thing, so just start off at Aweber would be my advice.
There's lots of other hosts: iContact, Constant Contact, and a bunch of
others. A lot of those have less restrictive terms of service, in terms of importing lists
than Aweber, so they can be good if you've got a big list that you've built somewhere
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 3
PLF_Module2v1.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 3 of 5 --

that you want to import. Importing that into Aweber can be a pain in the neck. In the
long run, it's probably worth it.
A lot of times at iContact and Constant Contact, you can import more easily. The
downside is the deliverability in general for those services is not as high at Aweber, and
one of the reasons is because they allow people to import lists. The thing with email
host services is that you ending up being lumped in with everyone else that is on that
service, in terms of your reputation, and getting your email delivered to different email
service providers. For example, people that subscribe to you will have accounts at Gmail
and every other account out there.
When you hit the send button, Gmail is looking at that email coming in, and if you’re
hosted at Aweber they might score you one way and if you're hosted at a different
service, they might score you a different way. Aweber is squeaky clean in terms of who
they allow on their service and so now, you're lumped in with these people as opposed
to maybe some other services that aren't as squeaky clean.
I know that I breezed over that pretty quick, but when you're looking at a service you
have to look at their long term reputation - iContact, Constant Contact, I haven't used
them, but there's a bunch of other ones you can use. If you're using them, that's great.
The one I feel best about is Aweber.
Then there's Ontraport. This is a little different type of a tool. It does list
management similar to Aweber, but it also has a shopping cart and affiliate tracking/
management built in. It's an extremely powerful tool. We started using it over a year
ago and we do love it. The link is: http://www.sixinseven.com/ontraport.
It is significantly more expensive than Aweber, but it does give you a lot more tools, a
lot more capabilities, and you really are stepping up into a different type of service here
where it's more of an all-in-one deal for you. I would definitely take a look at Ontraport.
It's probably not something that you're going to use when you're first starting out your
business, but then once you get more sophisticated, especially when you have multiple
lists, multiple products - it can be really powerful.
Then there’s 1ShoppingCart, this is also like Ontraport. It’s got integrated list
management, a shopping cart, and affiliate management. It’s not as sophisticated as
Ontraport, especially in what you can do with your email and your different email
follow-ups. It's just a whole different class. It's much simpler. It is much less expensive.
It's easier to use. I’m not a huge fan of their list management service. They've had
problems in the past getting delivered, not getting sent to the spam folder. They also
are a lot slower than Aweber, so you hit send and it can take a long time to send out
your email. If I was going to use 1ShoppingCart, I would probably use it in
tandem with Aweber. So I would use Aweber for the list management and I would
use 1ShoppingCart for the shopping cart and the affiliate management. Go to: http://
www.sixinseven.com/1sc.
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 4
PLF_Module1v1.0

-- 4 of 5 --

Infusionsoft. Very similar to Ontraport. Similar pricing to Ontraport. I've personally
never used it. I know a lot of people that do. It comes down to trying to decide
between Ontraport and Infusionsoft and there's a bit of an arms race between those
two in terms of different features, support, and pricing. I'd definitely take a look at both
of those if was going to go for that full-out pro solution. Infusionsoft, like Ontraport,
wouldn't be something that you'd get when you're first starting out. You can get there
at: http://www.sixinseven.com/infusionsoft.
Then there is Synergyx. This is a standalone shopping cart. I've used this one for
years as my shopping cart. It's not the most sophisticated in terms of features, but it is
a very solid shopping cart. Paul Galloway, who is the developer there, provides really
good support. I've been using them for more than a decade. Go to: http://
www.sixinseven.com/synergyx.
Those are some of the tools that I use for list management and affiliate
management. Synergyx will also do your affiliate management for you. I use Synergyx
for the shopping cart and affiliate management, and then Aweber for my email list.
Syngergyx is probably not a great “newbie” solution because it does go on your website
(and Paul Galloway will install it for you), but there is some maintenance that's going to
be involved. It’s just not all plug and play the way some of these other ones are.
Those are the tools that I've been using for years (and some other ones that are in the
market) that are highly recommended.
Again, Aweber, huge fan. Ontraport, big fan. 1ShoppingCart is a great place to start out
and Infusionsoft, another strong contender there.
